title: Consumers Report Higher Credit Rejection Rates, Expect Fewer Credit Applications

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The Federal Reserve Bank of New York's Survey of Consumer Expectations Credit Access Survey for October 2024 shows that reported rejection rates for credit applications increased across all categories in 2024, with mortgage refinances (25.6%) and auto loans (11.4%) reaching new series highs since the module began in 2013. The overall rejection rate rose to 21.0% from 20.1% in 2023, while lender-initiated account closures hit a new series high of 7.4%.
key_points: Overall credit application rejection rate increased to 21.0% in 2024 from 20.1% in 2023 and 18.0% in 2022, remaining well above the 2019 level of 17.6%
Mortgage application rejection rate rose 8.6 percentage points to 20.7% in 2024, well above the 2019 rate of 10.2%
Mortgage refinance rejection rate increased to a new series high of 25.6% in 2024 from 15.5% in 2023
Credit card limit increase rejection rate rose to 38.9% in 2024 from 30.9% in 2023
Lender-initiated account closures increased to 7.4% in 2024 from 7.2% in 2023, a new series high
Voluntary credit account closures dropped to 13.9% in 2024, a new series low
why_it_matters: Rising credit rejection rates signal tightening lending standards, which can slow consumer spending and refinancing activity. The shift from voluntary closures to lender-initiated closures indicates banks are becoming more proactive in managing credit risk. Higher expected rejection rates may deter credit applications, creating a feedback loop that further constrains credit availability and economic activity.
macro_context: The survey shows deteriorating credit access for households, particularly for mortgages and refinancing. Rising rejection rates across credit types suggest tighter financial conditions that could weigh on consumer spending and housing market activity. However, the slight improvement in ability to cover unexpected $2,000 expenses (66.5% vs 65.8%) indicates some resilience in household financial buffers.
market_structure_context: The shift toward lender-initiated account closures (7.4%, new series high) versus voluntary closures (13.9%, new series low) reflects changing dynamics in the credit market where financial institutions are taking more active steps to manage their loan portfolios and credit risk exposure.
important_numbers: 25.6% mortgage refinance rejection rate (new series high)
38.9% credit card limit increase rejection rate
11.4% auto loan rejection rate (new series high since 2013)
66.5% probability of obtaining $2,000 for unexpected need
notes: Source is from the New York Fed's Survey of Consumer Expectations Credit Access Survey, fielded in October 2024. Relevant to market structure as it captures credit market dynamics through rejection rates and lender behavior. Not a policy decision or rate announcement. Historical record (4.5 months old) but data remains relevant for understanding credit cycle trends. Survey is conducted every four months with annual press releases.
